title: Detecting Cycles in an Undirected Graph

statement.markdown:
Given an adjacency list representation of a graph, write a function to detect whether it contains a cycle using DFS. The graph is represented as an adjacency list where `graph[i]` is a list of all nodes that are directly connected to node `i`.

      The function should return `True` if the graph contains a cycle and `False` otherwise.

      You can assume that the graph is connected and does not have any self-loops.

      **Hint:** Use a visited set to keep track of visited nodes and their corresponding parents during the DFS traversal.
statement.inputFormat: An adjacency list representation of a graph where each edge is represented as a pair of nodes `[i, j]` in the input list.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the graph contains a cycle.

examples.explanation: Graph does not contain a cycle

hints.text: Use a visited set to keep track of visited nodes and their corresponding parents during the DFS traversal.

editorial.title: Detecting Cycles in an Undirected Graph
editorial.intuition: The idea is to perform a DFS traversal of the graph and check for backedges, which indicate the presence of a cycle.
commonMistakes: Missing the case where a node is visited but not yet marked as visited in the recursion stack.

functionSignature.java: public boolean detectCycle(int n, int[][] edges);
functionSignature.python: def detectCycle(self, n: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> bool;
